title: Housing sheep overnight: the decision
subtitle: What to weigh when deciding whether to bring sheep in at night, and when it makes practical sense.
excerpt: Bringing sheep in at night is not standard practice on most UK lowland farms, but on small mixed holdings with specific predator pressure, high-value stock, or welfare obligations during bad weather, the question comes up regularly. The decision has real costs on both sides: housing removes the grazing freedom that keeps sheep healthy, but it also removes some real risks.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Sheep (2010), gov.uk. AHDB Beef and Lamb: sheep housing and management. National Sheep Association guidance notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: Penning or housing sheep in a yard or covered building, typically overnight or during bad weather. Temporary yarding during severe weather is different from full-time housing; the distinction matters for ventilation requirements, bedding depth, and space allowances.

A group of conditions including pneumonia, pasteurellosis, and Mycoplasma ovipneumoniae infection that are strongly associated with poor ventilation in housed sheep. High ammonia, high humidity, and restricted airflow are the main risk factors. Pasteurellosis vaccine before housing reduces the risk significantly.

aliases: should I bring sheep in overnight

content.text: Most UK sheep spend most of their lives outdoors. The question of whether to bring them in overnight is less about sheep welfare and more about the specific risks on your land: predator pressure, weather severity, the value of the stock, and the management overhead of daily housing.
Arguments for overnight housing
Fox and dog predation on small flocks, particularly around lambing. A housed flock overnight removes the risk window when predators are most active.
In-lamb ewes approaching their due dates: housing allows close monitoring at night checks without a torch across a dark field.
Severe overnight winter weather: sheep can shelter under a good hedge from rain, but sleet on a newly shorn flock with no field shelter is a welfare risk.
Enabling the daily routine of feeding, handling, and checking to happen in a controlled space rather than open ground.
Arguments against overnight housing
Sheep in poorly ventilated housing develop
respiratory disease
. This is the main health risk of housing, driven by ammonia build-up, high humidity, and restricted airflow. Good ventilation reduces the risk but cannot remove it entirely.
Foot problems increase in housed animals on wet bedding. Foot rot and scald spread rapidly in a housed flock.
Daily
yarding
means housing the sheep each evening and letting them out each morning. Bedding must be kept fresh. Someone must be there at both ends of the day.
Grazing time lost to housing is grazing time the animal needs. Housed sheep need more hay and higher supplementary feed.
When regular overnight housing makes sense

Regular overnight housing makes most sense from six weeks before lambing through to weaning. Monitoring has real value during this window, and predation risk is at its highest for the most vulnerable animals. Housing in late pregnancy also helps prevent twin lamb disease, as the shepherd can ensure ewes are fed adequately through the night.
This is the housing model described in
housing in-lamb ewes before lambing
. The decision to extend that housing to the rest of the year is a different one.
Space and ventilation if you do house

The welfare code specifies minimum space requirements for housed sheep: 1.2 square metres per ewe for lowland breeds. The more important measure is ventilation: buildings need 0.1 square metres of open ridge vent per metre of building length, plus a generous eave opening on the windward side. A solid concrete-block building with only a small hinged doorway is a welfare problem for housed sheep, regardless of floor space.
